Exploring London's EU Referendum Vote through Ward based Census Data and Election Results¶

Part 2 - Geodemographic Classification and Cluster Analysis with R¶

  1. Picturing the Vote
  2. Cluster Analysis
  3. Comparing Geodemographic Classification and EU Referendum Vote
  4. Regional Context
  5. Political Context
  6. Swing Measurment
  7. Summary Visualisations
  • In the previous section our Machine Learning Analysis Workflow produced a set of Independent Variables that might be strongly associated with the Leave Vote. In this section we focus on the spatial context of this narrative through Cluster Analysis and visual comparison of the Regional & National Referendum result distribution.
  • We use the Jupyter IRKernel to incorporate R Functionality into the Jupyter Notebook format

Setup¶

In [ ]:
library(maptools)
library(RColorBrewer)
library(classInt)
library(OpenStreetMap)
library(sp)
library(rgeos)
library(tmap)
library(tmaptools)
library(sf)
library(rgdal)
library(geojsonio)
library(grid)
In [2]:
library(tidyverse)

-- Attaching packages --------------------------------------- tidyverse 1.2.1 --
v ggplot2 3.2.1     v purrr   0.3.2
v tibble  2.1.3     v dplyr   0.8.3
v tidyr   1.0.0     v stringr 1.4.0
v readr   1.3.1     v forcats 0.4.0
-- Conflicts ------------------------------------------ tidyverse_conflicts() --
x dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
x dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()

1 - Picturing the Vote¶

Leave vs Remain Maps¶

Load Ward ShapeFile¶

In [ ]:
#sink("output.txt")
WardMapSF1 <- read_shape(paste("C:\\Users\\Admin\\ml16\\",
        "Wards_December_2016_Generalised_Clipped_Boundaries_in_Great_Britain\\",
            "Wards_December_2016_Generalised_Clipped_Boundaries_in_Great_Britain.shp", sep=""))

Load Census and Election Results Data Model¶

In [ ]:
UKData2 <-read_csv("C:\\Users\\Admin\\ml16\\uk-ward-results-edit4.csv")
LondonData4 <- data.frame(UKData2)

SF to SP Conversion¶

In [6]:
library(methods)
#check the class of BoroughMapSF
class(WardMapSF1)
newSP <- as(WardMapSF1, "Spatial")
class(newSP)

  2. 'data.frame'

Colour Brewer Scales¶

In [31]:
display.brewer.all()
In [15]:
tmap_mode("plot")
tm_shape(LondonTrueWardsNewSF2) +
    tm_polygons(c("Remain", "Leave"),
                style=c("jenks", "pretty"),
        palette=list(seq = "YlOrBr" , seq = "Purples"),
                   ) +
    tm_facets(sync = FALSE, ncol = 2) +
    tm_legend(
          legend.position = c("right", "bottom"),
          legend.title.size = 1.3,
          legend.text.size = 0.6)  +
  tm_layout(asp=1, outer.margins=c(0, 0, 0, 0),
      inner.margins=0)

tmap mode set to plotting

2 - Creating a Basic Geodemographic Classification¶

In [20]:
LondonWards <- readOGR("C:\\Users\\Admin\\ml16\\NewLondonWard\\NewLondonWard.shp")
LondonWardsSF <- st_as_sf(LondonWards)
LondonWardsDF <- st_set_geometry(LondonWardsSF,NULL)

attach(LondonWardsSF)
attach(LondonWardsDF)

cbind(lapply(LondonWardsDF, class))

#clustering 
#Create a new data frame just containing the two variables we are
#interested in
mydata<-as.data.frame(LondonWardsDF[,c("PctAged65p","PctWithNoQ")])
attach(mydata)

Variable Check:¶

  • Ranged on the same scale
  • Normally distributed
  • Not highly correlated

Histogram¶

In [21]:
backup_options <- options()
library(repr)
# Change plot size to 4 x 3
options(repr.plot.width=4, repr.plot.height=3)

library(grid)
grid.newpage()
#pushViewport(viewport(width=1.8, height=0.9, layout=grid.layout(1,2)))
pushViewport(viewport(layout=grid.layout(1,2)))

histplot <- ggplot(data=mydata, aes(x=PctAged65p))
plot1 = histplot +geom_histogram()
print(plot1, vp=viewport(layout.pos.col = 1))
histplot <- ggplot(data=mydata, aes(x=PctWithNoQ))
plot2 = histplot +geom_histogram()
print(plot2, vp=viewport(layout.pos.col = 2))
#– check variable distributions first
#histplot <- ggplot(data=mydata, aes(x=PctOwned11))
#histplot +geom_histogram()

#histplot <- ggplot(data=mydata, aes(x= PctNoQl11))
#histplot +geom_histogram()

#histplot <- ggplot(data=mydata, aes(x=Pct65plus))
#histplot +geom_histogram()
#histplot <- ggplot(data=mydata, aes(x=PctBame11))
#hist
#Rest Options for Plot Size
options(backup_options)

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value with `binwidth`.
`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value with `binwidth`.

#backup_options <- options()
# Change plot size to 4 x 3
options(repr.plot.width=7, repr.plot.height=5)

library(corrplot)
corrmatrix <- cor(mydata)
#corrplot(corrmatrix, method = 'number')
corrplot(corrmatrix, method = "square", type = "upper", tl.col = "black", order = "hclust", col = brewer.pal(n = 5, name = "RdYlBu"))
options(backup_options)

corrplot 0.84 loaded

Cluster Selection¶

Some Options:

  • Silhouette
  • Elbow
  • Gap Static

Silhouette Score¶

https://medium.com/codesmart/r-series-k-means-clustering-silhouette-794774b46586

In [24]:
library(cluster) 
library(factoextra)

Welcome! Related Books: `Practical Guide To Cluster Analysis in R` at https://goo.gl/13EFCZ
In [25]:
silhouette_score <- function(k){
  km <- kmeans(df, centers = k, nstart=25)
  ss <- silhouette(km$cluster, dist(df))
  mean(ss[, 3])
}
k <- 2:10
avg_sil <- sapply(k, silhouette_score)
plot(k, type='b', avg_sil, xlab='Number of clusters', ylab='Average Silhouette Scores', frame=FALSE)

Note on K Means¶

In cluster analysis, the k-means algorithm can be used to partition the input data set into k partitions (clusters). However, the pure k-means algorithm is not very flexible, and as such is of limited use (except for when vector quantization as above is actually the desired use case). In particular, the parameter k is known to be hard to choose (as discussed above) when not given by external constraints. Another limitation is that it cannot be used with arbitrary distance functions or on non-numerical data. For these use cases, many other algorithms are superior.

Cluster Plot¶

In [26]:
# run a k-means to find 3 clusters – use 25 iterations
fit <- kmeans(mydata, 4, nstart=25) # 3 cluster solution
# get cluster means
centroid<-aggregate(mydata,by=list(fit$cluster),FUN=mean)
#print the results of the cluster groupings
centroid
Group.1PctAged65pPctWithNoQ
1 8.84128918.58465
2 10.41463210.37378
3 16.92917417.15339
4 11.92367926.52957
In [27]:
# as we only have variable two dimensions we can plot the clusters on a graph
p <- ggplot(mydata,aes(PctAged65p, PctWithNoQ))
p+geom_point(aes(colour=factor(fit$cluster)))+geom_point(data=centroid[,2:3],aes(PctAged65p, PctWithNoQ), size=7, shape=18)+ theme(legend.position="none")

Map of the GeoDemographic Classification¶

In [28]:
head(mydata)
PctAged65pPctWithNoQ
014.033898 6.7

ggplot¶

In [29]:
library(repr)
      
mydata$cluster <- fit$cluster

#add the cluster groups to the LondonWards data frame
LondonWardsSF$cluster<-mydata$cluster
#merge the cluster results into a fortified dataframe for plotting (we made LondonGeom earlier)
#London_geom<-merge(London_geom,LondonWardsDF,by.x="id", by.y="Wardcode1")
#now map our geodeomographic classification
map <- ggplot(LondonWardsSF) + geom_sf(mapping = aes(fill=cluster))+scale_fill_continuous(breaks=c(4,3,2,1), trans='reverse')
map

tmap¶

In [30]:
tm_shape(LondonWardsSF) +
              tm_polygons("cluster",
                          style="jenks",
                          palette="Purples",
                          legend.outside = TRUE, 
                          legend.outside.position = c("bottom", "top"),
                          auto.palette.mapping=FALSE,
                          title="Cluster")

"The argument auto.palette.mapping is deprecated. Please use midpoint for numeric data and stretch.palette for categorical data to control the palette mapping."

3 - Comparing Cluster with Election Result¶

Ref Results vs Geo Demo Class Facet Maps with R Grid Package¶

In [32]:
#library(grid)
grid.newpage()
pushViewport(viewport(layout=grid.layout(1,2)))


plot1 = tm_shape(LondonWardsSF) +
              tm_polygons("cluster",
                          style="jenks",
                          palette="Purples",
                          legend.outside = TRUE, 
                          legend.outside.position = c("bottom", "top"),
                          auto.palette.mapping=FALSE,
                          title="Cluster") +
    tm_legend(
          legend.position = c("right", "bottom"),
          legend.title.size = 0.5,
          legend.text.size = 0.4)  

plot2 = tm_shape(LondonTrueWardsNewSF2) +
              tm_polygons("Leave.",
                          style="jenks",
                          palette="Purples",
                          legend.outside = TRUE, 
                          legend.outside.position = c("bottom", "top"),
                          auto.palette.mapping=FALSE,
                          title="Leave")
    tm_legend(
          legend.position = c("right", "bottom"),
          legend.title.size = 0.5,
          legend.text.size = 0.4)  


print(plot1, vp=viewport(layout.pos.col = 1))
print(plot2, vp=viewport(layout.pos.col = 2))

4 - Regional Context¶

4.1 UK Ward Maps¶

In [ ]:
### Open Filter
UKWMap <- newSP

###  SP to SF Conversion
newSF2 <- st_as_sf(UKWMap)

nrow(newSF2)
head(newSF2)
head(UKWMap)

### Get UK Election Data

#Nulls Removed
UKData2 <-read_csv("C:\\Users\\Admin\\ml16\\uk-ward-results-edit2.csv")
WardData4 <- data.frame(UKData2)

### Join Data
UKWardsNewSF2 <- merge(newSF2, WardData4, by.x = "wd16cd", by.y = "WardCode", all=TRUE)

### Check Missing Data
nrow(UKWardsNewSF2)
sum(is.na(UKWardsNewSF2$Leave))

class(UKWardsNewSF2)
head(UKWardsNewSF2)
In [43]:
Plot1 <- qtm(UKWardsNewSF2, fill = "Remain.", fill.pallette = "Spectral" ) +
tm_legend(legend.position = c("left", "top"),
          main.title = "",
          main.title.position = "right")

Plot2 <- qtm(UKWardsNewSF2, fill = "Leave.", fill.pallette = "Purples" ) +
tm_legend(legend.position = c("left", "top"),
          main.title = "",
          main.title.position = "right")

Warning message:
"The shape UKWardsNewSF2 is invalid. See sf::st_is_valid"
In [44]:
tmap_mode("plot")

tmap mode set to plotting
In [45]:
library(grid)
grid.newpage()
pushViewport(viewport(layout=grid.layout(1,2)))

print(Plot1, vp=viewport(layout.pos.col = 1))
print(Plot2, vp=viewport(layout.pos.col = 2))

Note:¶

  • Highlight Missing Data from BBC Analysis
  • Freedom Of Infomation Request
  • Hard to visually assimilate ward based data on UK scale
  • The 1000 Wards that were collected by the Data Journalist Analysis via LA FOI request, look under represented here. Lets try an interactive map to check our wards have joined correcty, and/or to better assimilate the data.

4.2 UK Ward vs Borough Maps¶

In [46]:
BoroughMapSF <- read_shape("C:\\Users\\Admin\\ml16\\England_lad_2011\\england_lad_2011.shp", as.sf = TRUE)

head(BoroughMapSF)

class(BoroughMapSF)

### Join on Borough Code

UKBoroughsNewSF <- merge(BoroughMapSF, UKBorough3, by.x = "name", by.y = "CountingArea", all=TRUE)

head(UKBoroughsNewSF)

sum(is.na(UKBoroughsNewSF$Leave))

4.3 UK Borough Leave vs Remain Maps¶

In [66]:
tmap_mode("plot")

plot1 = tm_shape(UKBoroughsNewSF) +
              tm_polygons("Leave.",
                          style="jenks",
                          palette="Purples",
                          #auto.palette.mapping=FALSE,
                          title="Leave") +
    tm_legend(
          legend.position = c("left", "top"),
          legend.title.size = 0.5,
          legend.text.size = 0.4)  

plot2 = tm_shape(UKBoroughsNewSF) +
              tm_polygons("Remain.",
                          style="jenks",
                          palette="YlOrBr",
                          #auto.palette.mapping=FALSE,
                          title="Remaine") +
    tm_legend(
          legend.position = c("left", "top"),
          legend.title.size = 0.5,
          legend.text.size = 0.4) 




#library(grid)
grid.newpage()
pushViewport(viewport(layout=grid.layout(1,2)))
print(plot1, vp=viewport(layout.pos.col = 1, layout.pos.row = 1))
print(plot2, vp=viewport(layout.pos.col = 2, layout.pos.row = 1))

### Display Interactive London Borough Map

tmap mode set to plotting

5 - Political Context and Background¶

In [ ]:
UKData_Contxt <-read_csv("C:\\Users\\Admin\\ml11\\DSS_Coursework_ASutton\\final_data.csv")
LondonData5 <- data.frame(UKData_Contxt)

LondonTrueWardsNewSF3 <- merge(LondonWMap, LondonData5, by.x = "wd16cd", by.y = "WardCode", all=TRUE)
head(LondonTrueWardsNewSF3)
In [19]:
tmap_mode("plot")
tm_shape(LondonTrueWardsNewSF3) +
    tm_polygons(c("Leave_Percent", "Council_Party"),
                style=c("jenks", "pretty"),
        palette=list(seq = "Purples" , seq = "Purples"),
                   ) +
    tm_facets(sync = FALSE, ncol = 2) +
    tm_legend(
          legend.position = c("right", "bottom"),
          legend.title.size = 0.9,
          legend.text.size = 0.6)  +
  tm_layout(asp=1, outer.margins=c(0, 0, 0, 0),
      inner.margins=0)

tm_shape(LondonTrueWardsNewSF3) +
    tm_polygons(c("Remain_Percent", "Council_Party"),
                style=c("jenks", "pretty"),
        palette=list(seq = "YlOrBr", c("blue", "orange", "red", "yellow", "brown", "purple", "grey")),
                   ) +
    tm_facets(sync = FALSE, ncol = 2) +
    tm_legend(
          legend.position = c("right", "bottom"),
          legend.title.size = 0.9,
          legend.text.size = 0.6)  +
  tm_layout(asp=1, outer.margins=c(0, 0, 0, 0),
      inner.margins=0)

tm_shape(LondonTrueWardsNewSF3) +
    tm_polygons(c( "London_Mayor_", "Constituency_Party"),
                style=c("jenks", "pretty"),
        palette=list(c("blue", "red", "grey"), c("blue", "red", "grey") ),
                   ) +
    tm_facets(sync = FALSE, ncol = 2) +
    tm_legend(
          legend.position = c("right", "bottom"),
          legend.title.size = 0.9,
          legend.text.size = 0.6)  +
  tm_layout(asp=1, outer.margins=c(0, 0, 0, 0),
      inner.margins=0)

tmap mode set to plotting

Interactive Maps¶

In [ ]:
#tmap_mode("view")
tm_shape(LondonTrueWardsNewSF3) +
    tm_polygons(c( "Constituency_Party", "Gain_Hold"),
                popup.vars="WardName",
                style=c("jenks", "pretty"),
                palette=list(c("blue", "red", "grey"), c("blue", "brown", "yellow", "red", "grey") ),
               ) +
    tm_facets(sync = FALSE, ncol = 2) +
    tm_legend(
          legend.position = c("right", "bottom"),
          legend.title.size = 0.9,
          legend.text.size = 0.6)  +
  tm_layout(asp=1, outer.margins=c(0, 0, 0, 0),
      inner.margins=0)
  • Issue with Embedded Interactive Maps in IRKernal

Click here to Launch Interactive TMap Example in New Tab

6 - Swing Measurement and Ideological Polarisation in Party Systems¶

“The statistical measure by which the switch of voters from one party to another on a national or constituency basis can be judged. It is calculated by adding the rise in one party’s vote to the fall of the other, and dividing by two.”

Brewer’s Politics (published by Cassell) Nicholas Comfort describes swing in the following terms:
